key_insight: Finite element modeling combined with hardness-based property estimation shows that the 36-year-operated X52 pipeline weld has a higher local stress concentration than the as-received weld.
engineering_relevance: This provides a practical way to assess whether aged gas-pipeline welds remain suitable for continued service or repurposing to hydrogen transport.
method: Measured hardness across weld metal, heat-affected zone, and base metal.
Estimated strength and plasticity characteristics from hardness correlations.
Built FEM welded-joint models using zone-specific stress-strain curves and simulated tensile loading.
evidence_signal: The operated weld reached a higher normalized stress concentration (σ11/σys = 1.25) than the as-received joint (σ11/σys = 1.1), with local strain increase occurring at lower loads.
watch_item: Hardness-derived material properties and FEM predictions should be validated carefully against additional experimental datasets before using them for fitness-for-service decisions.
paper_title: Assessment of the Strength of Welded Joints in a Gas Pipeline Using FEM Modeling
